Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study on certain days, but then I'd just... not. Most of my 16 hours happened in random bursts, usually late at night when the anxiety got bad enough that I couldn't ignore things anymore. I'd watch videos or flip through materials without any real plan, just trying to feel like I was doing something.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly panic when I remembered a deadline and cram everything into a few hours. Regular study times felt impossible to maintain—something always came up, or I just couldn't bring myself to start. The inconsistency made everything feel harder than it needed to be.
Q3: Usually it was fear, honestly. I'd get a notification about a deadline or remember that I hadn't logged in for a while, and the anxiety would push me to check in. Sometimes I logged in just to see what I'd missed, which usually made me feel worse. It wasn't excitement about learning—it was more like damage control.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, logging in every other day. But it never stuck. Life stuff got in the way, I'd miss a few days, and then logging back in felt overwhelming because I'd fallen behind. The routine just never formed for me.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful because I'm a visual learner and I could actually see concepts being explained. I watched quite a bit of video content—it was easier to absorb than reading dense text. When the videos had clear diagrams or step-by-step visuals, I felt like I was actually learning something. That's about the only thing that consistently worked for me.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely. I only posted three times the whole course, and I never interacted with other students. It felt pointless—like, what was I supposed to say? I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because I didn't have money for textbooks and the online resources felt like too much effort to dig through.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or understand how it connected to the exam. If something was going to help me pass, I'd do it. But if it felt like busywork or I couldn't see the point, I'd put it off indefinitely. With limited energy and resources, I had to pick my battles.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist for me. If something wasn't required, I wasn't doing it—I barely had the energy for the mandatory stuff. Required activities felt like obligations I was constantly failing to meet, while optional ones just added to the pile of things I was ignoring. The guilt was the same either way.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of lecture slides and videos to my phone so I could look at them without using data or needing to log in. I'd review stuff on the bus or when I couldn't sleep at night. I also watched some YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials. None of that would show up in the system.
Q10: There were nights when I'd spend hours going through downloaded slides, trying to understand concepts, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also think about course material a lot—like, turning problems over in my head while doing other things. Mental engagement doesn't leave a trace, but it's still studying in a way.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a video or PDF once, then never open it in the LMS again. The files lived on my phone, and I'd return to them multiple times. My actual engagement with those materials was way higher than what my login history would suggest. It was just easier to study offline when I could.
Q12: There were several points where I just couldn't engage anymore. When my anxiety spiked, everything felt impossible—even opening the LMS felt like too much. I'd also hit walls when I fell too far behind and catching up seemed pointless. The sadness would creep in and I'd think, what's the point of trying now?
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. I was juggling stuff with limited resources, so sometimes I had to prioritize survival over studying. When I was exhausted or confused by the material, the LMS became this thing I avoided because it reminded me of all the ways I was failing. The competing responsibilities never stopped.
Q14: Oh, all the time. I'd have the LMS open, videos playing, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd watched twenty minutes of a lecture and absorbed nothing. The anxiety made it hard to concentrate—I was physically present in the materials but mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The bigger the assignment, the more I avoided it. It's like my brain couldn't handle the pressure, so it just... shut down. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd feel even worse about it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me act. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was breathing down my neck, then scramble to submit something. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined in between. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could function.
Q17: I didn't really engage much with feedback. When I did get it, I'd either feel too anxious to read it carefully or too defeated to apply it. Good feedback might motivate me briefly, but bad feedback just confirmed what I already believed—that I wasn't good enough. So I mostly avoided checking.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with seven online courses happening at once. I couldn't keep track of what was due when, and the lack of clear priorities made everything blur together. A more guided structure with fewer choices might have helped me stay on track, but instead I just drifted.
Q19: The flexibility was supposed to be a good thing, but for me it was a trap. Without rigid deadlines or mandatory check-ins, I'd let things slide. The discussion requirements felt forced and unhelpful. What would have helped is more visual content and clearer, simpler pathways through the material.
Q20: I think that happens when someone is trying to engage but can't convert that effort into output. Like, they're logging in, watching videos, maybe even taking notes—but when it comes to actually producing work, something blocks them. Anxiety, confusion, life circumstances. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That's kind of me, I guess. I passed with decent exam scores despite barely showing up in the LMS. I studied offline a lot, and I'm okay at cramming when I have to be. The system doesn't capture what happens outside of it, so someone like me looks disengaged when really I'm just engaging differently.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked things and how long pages were open. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention, or if you were learning from downloaded materials, or if you were struggling emotionally while staring at the screen. They capture presence, not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in consistent effort. For me, I wanted to be engaged, but I couldn't sustain it. Real engagement would mean showing up regularly, participating, and feeling connected to the material. I had moments of that, but mostly I was just surviving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling internally—the anxiety that made logging in feel terrifying, the sadness that made everything pointless, the offline studying that kept me barely afloat. It wouldn't show that I wanted to do better but couldn't figure out how. The human stuff gets lost in the data.
